content.text: Every cloud workload lives in one of four deployment contexts: public cloud, private cloud, hybrid cloud, or multicloud. These are not competing options with a single winner — they address different constraints, and most large organizations use more than one simultaneously.

For the AZ-900 exam, you need to define each model, identify its key characteristics, and understand when each is appropriate. In practice, you also need to know when a stated requirement rules one out.

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison of Public, Private, Hybrid, and Multicloud deployment models across ownership, infrastructure, cost, elasticity, data control, compliance, maintenance, and best-use dimensions
attrs.title: Cloud Deployment Model Comparison

content.text: Cloud infrastructure owned and operated by a provider, shared among many tenants over the internet.
Azure VMs, AKS, Azure SQL, Azure Functions, all managed services
Cloud infrastructure dedicated to one organization, on-premises or in a hosted facility, providing cloud-like elasticity and self-service.
Azure Stack HCI, Azure Stack Hub, Azure Stack Edge, Azure VMware Solution
A combination of on-premises or private cloud with a public cloud, connected so that data and applications can flow between them.
Azure Arc, ExpressRoute, Azure File Sync, Azure Site Recovery, Entra Connect
Using cloud services from two or more public cloud providers simultaneously.
Azure Arc (managing EKS/GKE), Defender for Cloud (AWS/GCP connectors), Azure Data Factory (cross-cloud pipelines)
Uncontrolled consumption; idle resources not turned off
Over-provisioning for peak; hardware refresh cycles
Egress fees; managing two billing systems
Cross-cloud egress; reduced volume discounts per provider
Provider owns physical + hypervisor; you own OS up
You own all layers — physical through app
Self-certification + customer audits of your facility
Split — private layers owned, cloud layers shared
Both provider certifications and on-prem audit
Widest scope — all providers must be audited
Virtually unlimited horizontal scaling. Azure VMSS scales from 1 to 1,000 VMs in minutes. No pre-provisioning required. Global CDN via Azure Front Door puts content within 10 ms of any user.

Bounded by physical capacity. Scaling requires procuring and racking hardware — lead times of weeks or months. Over-provisioning is common (average on-premises server utilization is 15–20%). Predictable performance with no noisy-neighbor risk.

Steady-state capacity on-premises (CapEx sunk) with elastic burst to public cloud for peaks. The on-premises tier has predictable latency; cloud tier is elastic. The network link between them (ExpressRoute, VPN) is the bottleneck to design around.

Each cloud independently scalable. The challenge is that cross-cloud latency (10–50 ms even with direct interconnects) constrains synchronous service-to-service calls. Design for async where performance matters.
PowerShell DSC, Azure Stack ARM templates
Bicep (Azure) + ARM for Stack HCI; Arc GitOps for K8s
Decision Framework: Choosing the Right Model
Work through these questions in order. The first constraint that applies determines the minimum model.
Is data physically prohibited from leaving your premises or country (air-gap, export control, hard sovereignty law)?
→ Private Cloud only (Azure Stack Hub for air-gapped; Azure Stack HCI for connected private).
Do you have significant on-premises hardware still within its amortization period that cannot be retired?
→ Hybrid Cloud: run steady-state on-premises, new workloads in public cloud.
Does a specific non-Azure cloud service provide material advantage for a critical workload (BigQuery, SageMaker, Vertex AI)?
→ Multicloud for that workload, managed via Azure Arc.
→ Public Cloud (Azure). Simplest to operate, fastest time to market, lowest total infrastructure cost for new workloads.

These models are not mutually exclusive within an organization. A bank might use public cloud for its customer-facing app tier, private cloud (Azure Stack HCI) for core banking systems in its data center, hybrid connectivity via ExpressRoute, and a specific AWS service for ML model training. All four models, all appropriate, all within the same enterprise.
No CapEx, fast scaling, no ops team for infrastructure
Classified data cannot touch public internet; Azure Stack Hub
Steady-state on-prem (existing investment), burst to Azure for Black Friday / seasonal peaks
PHI data sovereignty on-prem + Azure for analytics, AI diagnostics (HIPAA BAA)
Acquired companies on different clouds; Azure for primary workloads, AWS for ML, GCP for analytics
SCADA runs on-prem (< 1 ms latency), telemetry and ML inference in Azure IoT Hub + Stream Analytics
Organizations rarely start with their final deployment model. The typical evolution:

Install ExpressRoute or VPN Gateway. Deploy Azure Arc agents on on-premises servers. Start running new workloads in Azure while existing workloads remain on-premises. This is the most common enterprise cloud journey (Lift-and-Shift phases 1–3).

Gradually migrate on-premises workloads to Azure services as hardware refreshes come due. Use Azure Migrate to assess each workload. After the last data center workload migrates, decommission the on-premises tier and the ExpressRoute circuit.

An acquisition brings a company running on AWS or GCP. Immediate action: Arc-enable the foreign clusters, connect to Defender for Cloud, import billing. Long-term: evaluate which workloads to migrate to Azure vs. run in place permanently.

Decide to use a specific non-Azure service (e.g., BigQuery for analytics, SageMaker for ML). Deploy Azure Arc on the foreign clusters from the start. Design data flows to minimize cross-cloud egress. Establish governance via Defender for Cloud connectors.
The AZ-900 exam tests your ability to identify the right model for a given scenario. The question types follow a pattern:
Scenario: "A company has data that must remain on-premises."
→ Private cloud or hybrid cloud. If they also need public services: hybrid.
Scenario: "A startup wants to minimize capital expenses."
Scenario: "A company uses Azure for most workloads but also uses AWS for a specific service."
Scenario: "A company wants to use Azure for cloud workloads and keep some servers in their own datacenter."
Scenario: "Which model provides the most control over data?"
Scenario: "Which model allows existing on-premises investment to be preserved while adopting cloud?"
Not necessarily. A private cloud can be hosted in a colocation facility — the defining attribute is dedicated (single-tenant) infrastructure, not physical location.
"Hybrid cloud is just cloud migration in progress."
Some organizations will remain hybrid permanently because of compliance, latency, or economics. Hybrid is a valid long-term architecture, not a transitional state.
Running the same workload across two providers is expensive to build and maintain. Most multicloud deployments are workload-partitioned (different apps on different clouds) rather than redundant.
"Public cloud is less secure than private cloud."

Hyperscale providers invest billions in physical security, hardware root of trust, and compliance. Most cloud security incidents stem from misconfiguration (too-permissive storage, missing MFA) — not provider vulnerabilities. A well-configured public cloud is more secure than a typical self-managed data center.
Same Workload in Each Deployment Model

To make the comparison concrete, consider a web application (REST API + database) deployed in each model. The application logic is identical; only the infrastructure and operational responsibilities differ.
Public Cloud: Azure Container Apps + Azure SQL
In public cloud, you provision managed services. No servers to manage — the provider handles OS patching, HA, and scaling. You write a Bicep template, deploy, and focus on application code.
Private Cloud: Azure Stack HCI + SQL Server VM

In private cloud, you manage the infrastructure. The same application runs inside VMs on your HCI cluster. You provision, patch, and HA-configure everything. Bicep-like templates exist for Stack HCI, but you add the operational layer.

# Private cloud: Azure Stack HCI cluster
# Create a VM for the API tier (managed by you, not the provider)
New-AzStackHCIVm `
  -Name "api-vm-01" `
  -ResourceGroupName "on-prem-rg" `
  -Location "eastus" \ `
  -CustomLocationId "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/hci-rg/providers/Microsoft.ExtendedLocation/customLocations/hci-cl" `
  -VmSize "Standard_D4s_v3" `
  -OsType "Linux" `
  -ImageId "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/hci-rg/providers/Microsoft.AzureStackHCI/galleries/hci-gallery/images/ubuntu-22.04"

# SQL Server VM (you manage SQL patches, backups, HA with Always On AG)
New-AzStackHCIVm `
  -Name "sql-vm-01" `
  -ResourceGroupName "on-prem-rg" `
  ...
  -OsType "Windows"

# You are responsible for:
# - OS patching (Patch Tuesday, kernel updates)
# - SQL Server installation and licensing
# - Always On Availability Group configuration for HA
# - Backup schedules and restore testing
# - Certificate management for TLS
Hybrid Cloud: On-Prem Database + Azure API Tier

In hybrid, the sensitive data stays on-premises (data residency requirement) while the stateless API tier runs in Azure and scales elastically. The key engineering challenge is the network path between tiers.

In multicloud, the database runs on AWS (perhaps due to an acquisition that brought an existing AWS RDS instance) while the API layer runs on Azure. Cross-cloud latency and egress fees are the key constraints.

# Multicloud: Python API on Azure Container Apps calling AWS RDS PostgreSQL
# Authentication: Workload Identity Federation (Azure Managed Identity -> AWS)
# Network: Azure -> Internet/Direct Connect -> AWS VPC -> RDS
import asyncpg
import boto3
from azure.identity import ManagedIdentityCredential
from azure.keyvault.secrets import SecretClient
import asyncio

# Store AWS RDS credentials in Azure Key Vault (not in code or env vars)
cred = ManagedIdentityCredential()
kv_client = SecretClient(vault_url="https://mykv.vault.azure.net", credential=cred)

async def get_db_pool():
    # Retrieve RDS password from Key Vault at startup
    password = kv_client.get_secret("aws-rds-password").value
    
    # Connect to AWS RDS PostgreSQL via its public endpoint
    # (Or via a private Direct Connect + Transit Gateway path for lower latency)
    pool = await asyncpg.create_pool(
        host="prod-db.xxxx.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com",
        port=5432,
        user="apiuser",
        password=password,
        database="orders",
        # SSL required for RDS
        ssl="require",
        min_size=5,
        max_size=20,
        # Longer timeouts: cross-cloud RTT is ~20ms vs ~1ms within a cloud
        command_timeout=30.0,
    )
    return pool

# Design principle: batch DB calls to minimize cross-cloud round-trips
# A chatty API (10 sequential DB calls per request) compounds 20ms RTT -> 200ms per request
# Solution: use batch queries, read-replicas in Azure region if available, or cache with Redis

TCO comparisons between deployment models are frequently misrepresented because they compare only hardware/compute costs while ignoring staff, facilities, operational overhead, and opportunity cost. A complete TCO must include:
On-prem licenses; Azure Hybrid Benefit saves ~40%
High — pay only for what you use

For a 500-VM equivalent workload, Microsoft's own TCO Calculator typically shows on-premises costing 2–3x Azure public cloud over a 5-year period when all factors above are included. The gap narrows (or reverses) only for very large, stable, well-utilized private infrastructure with existing software licenses and long-term CapEx already committed.
Different regulatory frameworks mandate different deployment model constraints. Understanding which regulations apply to your workload often decides the model before technical considerations arise.
Azure has a HIPAA Business Associate Agreement. All major Azure regions qualify. Public cloud is widely used for healthcare.
Azure is PCI DSS certified. Reduce cardholder data scope using tokenization; minimize what enters Azure.
Use Azure EU regions + data residency commitments. Microsoft's Data Processing Agreement (DPA) covers GDPR. Standard Contractual Clauses for US transfers.
Azure Government cloud (us-gov-virginia, us-gov-arizona) is FedRAMP High authorized. Not available in commercial regions.
Requires Azure Stack Hub (disconnected mode) or Azure Government Secret/Top Secret clouds with physical isolation.
Azure is ISO 27001 certified. Inherit provider controls in your own certification scope.
Requires multi-region architecture and documented exit strategy. Azure's resilience SLA and audit rights cover core requirements.
Microsoft has specific products for each deployment model. Knowing which product belongs in which model category is a common AZ-900 test pattern.
IaaS — you manage OS and above; Azure manages the hypervisor, host hardware, and physical datacenter.
Managed Kubernetes — Azure manages the control plane; you manage node pools and workloads.
Fully managed PaaS — no OS, no SQL Server instance to manage. 99.99% SLA with automatic failover.
Serverless — no VMs, no containers to manage. Scale to zero when idle.
Hyper-converged infrastructure running a subset of Azure services on your hardware in your datacenter. Runs VMs, AKS, and Azure Arc extensions on-premises.
A full Azure region in a rack — runs ARM, Azure Portal, Azure Marketplace images. Supports fully disconnected (air-gapped) operation for classified environments.
GPU-accelerated edge appliance. Runs Azure IoT Edge modules and ML inference at the network edge (factory, store, remote site).
Runs VMware workloads on Azure-dedicated bare metal in Azure datacenters. Migrates vSphere VMs without re-platforming.
Projects on-premises and multi-cloud resources into ARM. Enables Azure Policy, RBAC, Defender, and Update Manager on non-Azure resources.
Dedicated private connectivity from on-premises to Azure. Up to 100 Gbps, 99.95% SLA, traffic does not traverse the public internet.
IPsec/IKEv2 VPN tunnel over internet. Up to 10 Gbps (VpnGw5). Lower cost than ExpressRoute but higher latency.
Synchronizes on-premises Active Directory accounts to Entra ID for hybrid SSO.
Caches Azure Files shares on Windows Server, enabling on-premises access at LAN speeds with cloud backup and sync.
Replicates on-premises VMs to Azure for disaster recovery. RPO minutes, RTO hours without a second data center.
Registers EKS, GKE, and any CNCF cluster with ARM for unified policy, RBAC, and GitOps.
Connects AWS accounts and GCP projects for unified Secure Score and threat detection.
90+ connectors for cross-cloud data pipelines (S3, GCS, BigQuery, Redshift, Snowflake, etc.).
Trusts OIDC tokens from AWS IAM, GCP Workload Identity, and GitHub Actions — enabling secretless cross-cloud service authentication.
The availability SLA you can achieve differs significantly by deployment model. Public cloud provides the highest SLAs for managed services; private cloud SLA depends entirely on how you design it.
Built-in Always On (3 replicas), zone redundant
VMs in separate physical zones; load balancer
VMs in different fault/update domains in same datacenter
Storage Spaces Direct, Hyper-V live migration, witness VM
Depends on on-prem DB + ExpressRoute SLA

In hybrid and multicloud, composite SLA = product of each component's SLA. A 99.99% Azure tier connected to a 99.9% on-premises DB over a 99.95% ExpressRoute gives composite SLA of 99.99% × 99.9% × 99.95% = 99.84%. Design the weakest link first.
Public cloud: provider-owned, shared multi-tenant, OpEx, unlimited elasticity — best for new workloads, startups, and cost-elastic demand.
Private cloud: organization-owned or dedicated, single-tenant, CapEx-heavy, bounded elasticity — best for air-gapped, regulated, or sovereignty-constrained workloads.
Hybrid cloud: connects on-premises to public cloud — enables incremental migration, burst capacity, and meeting both sovereignty and elasticity requirements simultaneously.
Multicloud: uses multiple public providers — enables best-of-breed services and geographic coverage, at the cost of higher operational complexity and fragmented billing.
These models are complementary, not mutually exclusive. Large enterprises typically combine all four for different workloads.

For AZ-900: the decision criteria are data control (private > hybrid > public), elasticity (public > hybrid burst > private), cost model (public OpEx vs. private CapEx), and management complexity (public lowest, multicloud highest).
